build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: The player views surveillance footage on a CRT monitor with play, pause, rewind, and fast-forward controls. A timestamp counter runs during playback. The footage shows scenes with activity that the player can scrub through. Score 0 if there is no footage playback, if there are no transport controls, or if there is no visible timestamp.

The player can mark anomalies by pausing at the right moment and clicking a 'Mark Anomaly' button. Correct marks earn investigation points with positive feedback. False marks cost sanity with negative feedback. The system distinguishes correct from incorrect marks. Score 0 if there is no marking mechanic, if all marks are treated equally, or if there is no feedback on correctness.

A sanity meter depletes when viewing anomalies and when making false marks. Low sanity causes visual corruption: screen distortion, phantom elements in footage, and false anomalies that trick the player. At zero sanity, the session ends. Score 0 if there is no sanity system, if sanity never depletes, or if low sanity has no visual effect.

At least four distinct surveillance tapes exist, each covering a different camera location (hallway, lab, storage, courtyard). Each tape has different footage content, anomaly types, and visual style. Tapes are selectable from a shelf or menu. Score 0 if there are fewer than 4 tapes, if tapes show identical content, or if there is no selection interface.

At least three distinct anomaly types exist across tapes: shadow movement, object disappearance, figure appearance, light flicker, or spatial distortion. Each anomaly type has a unique visual presentation. Anomalies are subtle enough to require attention. Score 0 if there are fewer than 3 anomaly types, if anomalies are identical, or if they are too obvious (full-screen effects).

An investigation board or case file connects marked anomalies across tapes. Correctly marking related anomalies reveals connections and advances the story. The board visually fills in as progress is made. Score 0 if there is no investigation tracking, if anomalies are unconnected, or if there is no visual progress display.

The archive room environment changes as the investigation progresses: objects shift, new items appear, the lighting changes, and the room becomes increasingly unsettling. At least three distinct room state changes occur. Score 0 if the room never changes, if changes are not tied to progress, or if fewer than 3 changes occur.

The full review loop is connected: Select tape, watch footage, spot anomaly, pause and mark, receive feedback, update investigation board. Each step produces visible state changes. Score 0 if any step in the chain is missing or broken.

The full game loop is connected: Title screen, archive room, select tape, review footage, mark anomalies, manage sanity, advance investigation, reach conclusion. The player can experience every node without restarting the application. Score 0 if any transition in the chain is missing or broken.

A title screen is present and styled: it shows the game name in a VHS or monospace font with tracking-line effects, has at minimum a play button that is interactive and transitions to the game, and uses a non-default background (static, tape imagery, or surveillance aesthetic) — not plain Godot grey or a solid-colour fill. Score 0 if the game launches directly into gameplay with no title screen, or if the start button is non-functional.

Overall art-style coherence: every visible element — CRT monitor, archive room, tape shelf, investigation board, UI controls — belongs to a single consistent retro-surveillance visual language. The palette is dim with CRT-green or amber highlights; no clashing bright colours or unrelated assets. Score 0 if the screen mixes incompatible styles, or if elements are obviously unthemed placeholder shapes.

Environment, character, and UI art quality: the archive room uses detailed illustrated surfaces; the CRT monitor has a convincing screen effect; tape controls look functional; UI elements use styled, themed widgets — not raw unstyled Godot defaults. Score 0 if the room is a uniform colour fill, if the monitor is a plain rectangle, or if primary UI elements are obviously unstyled default widgets.

Animation continuity and polish: tape playback, anomaly appearances, marking feedback, sanity corruption effects, room state changes, and investigation board updates each play as a smooth multi-frame tween or effect — no element snaps to its new state in a single frame. Scene transitions use a brief fade rather than an instant cut. Score 0 if any of these moments is instantaneous, or if transitions are hard cuts with no tween.
